summary:
The patient may have a false-positive result from an interference such as medication or dietary component leading to a reactive Benedict test despite normoglycemia detected via more specific testing techniques. Understanding both the limitations inherent within different diagnostic tools and recognizing common causes for atypical results helps guide further investigation into possible non-glycosuria-related explanations.
The most probable explanation involves substances acting similarly to sugars under Benedict reagent conditions without actually being elevated serum glucose levels; thus necessitating additional confirmatory diagnostics beyond just urinalysis alone. To pinpoint exact causality would require detailed history taking regarding recent drug intake alongside possibly repeating biochemical assays targeting direct measurement rather than indirect detection through color change reactions used here. Agent1 concludes considering multiple plausible scenarios but leans towards identifying external influences affecting Benedict reaction accuracy over pathologic hyperglycaemic states indicated initially based solely upon presumptive readings obtained using less precise methodologies compared against gold standard laboratory determinations directly measuring plasma concentrations instead relying purely off visual changes observed during chemical interactions between sample contents versus expected standards set forth beforehand when formulating hypotheses about underlying physiological processes potentially driving abnormal findings noted clinically today among patients presenting similar symptoms warranting thorough evaluation before definitive conclusions drawn definitively linking them back specifically toward diabetes mellitus diagnosis given current available information presented herein limited strictly speaking only focusing around differential etiologies capable explaining away apparent discrepancies seen across various modalities employed sequentially throughout work-up process conducted so far up until now inclusive thereof. Summary:

A false-positive Benedict test could be caused by substance interferences like some medications or foods, while the normal glucose level suggests no significant glycolytic issues. Further confirmation through direct glucose measurements is necessary to rule out misleading results from the urinary test.
